In General – Except as provided in Plan Section 7.19(B), this Plan is a Top-Heavy Plan if any of the following conditions exist:

1. if the top-heavy ratio for this Plan exceeds 60 percent and this Plan is not part of any Required Aggregation Group or Permissive Aggregation Group of plans;
2. if this Plan is part of a Required Aggregation Group of plans but not part of a Permissive Aggregation Group and the top-heavy ratio for the group of plans exceeds 60 percent; or
3. if this Plan is a part of a Required Aggregation Group and part of a Permissive Aggregation Group of plans and the top-heavy ratio for the Permissive Aggregation Group of plans and the top-heavy ratio for the Permissive Aggregation Group of plans exceeds 60 percent. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]

relius_provision_text:
Notwithstanding anything in this Section to the contrary, effective for mid-year amendments adopted after January 28, 2016, the Employer may amend the Plan effective on a date other than the first day of the Plan Year, or with a retroactive effective date, so long as the amendment complies with IRS Notice 2016-16 and other IRS Guidance. For permissible mid-year Plan changes impacting a Plan's required safe harbor notice content, a notice will be provided to all Eligible Employees pursuant to IRS Notice 2016-16 or any later IRS Guidance.

Each employee required to be provided an updated safe harbor notice pursuant to Notice 2016-16 must be given a reasonable opportunity (including a reasonable period after receipt of the updated notice) before the effective date of the mid-year change to change the employee's cash or deferred election (and/or any after-tax employee contribution election). For this purpose, a 30-day election period is deemed to be a reasonable period to make or change a cash or deferred election. If it is not practicable for the election opportunity to be provided before the effective date of the change (for example, in the case of a mid-year change to increase matching contributions retroactively for the entire plan year), an employee is treated as having a reasonable opportunity to make or change an election if the election opportunity begins as soon as practicable after the date the updated notice is provided to the employee, but not later than 30 days after the date the change is adopted. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: QACA Notice Requirement – A comprehensive notice of the Employee's rights and obligations under the Plan, written in a manner calculated to be understood by the average Eligible Employee which meets the content requirements of Code section 401(k)(13) and its associated regulations and other guidance, will be provided to affected Participants within a reasonable period of time before the start of the first Plan Year in which the QACA provisions become effective and before each subsequent Plan Year. In addition to the requirements found in Treasury Regulation section 1.401(k)-3(d), the notice will accurately describe (1) the amount of the default Elective Deferrals that will be made on the Employee's behalf, (b) the Employee's right to elect to have a different Elective Deferral withheld including the right to not make Elective Deferrals at all, and (c) how Elective Deferral will be invested if the Employee does not provide investment instructions. A period of 30 to 90 days before the beginning of the Plan Year is deemed to be a reasonable period. Whether a different period is reasonable will be determined based on all of the relevant facts and circumstances. If a Plan has an eligibility period of less than 30 days (e.g., immediate eligibility), the Plan can provide the notice to Participants when they become eligible. If notice cannot be provided on or before the Employee's eligibility date, it will be deemed timely if it is provided as soon as practicable after that date and before the pay date for the payroll period in which the Employee becomes eligible. In such case, the Employee must be allowed to defer from Compensation earned beginning on the date the Employee enters the Plan. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions relate to eligibility and notice requirements for plan participants, but they govern different aspects of eligibility and timing for notices. The Relius provision focuses on mid-year amendments and the timing of elections related to those changes, while the Ascensus provision addresses the QACA notice requirements and the timing of notices related to the start of the Plan Year.
variance_notes: Key differences include the focus on mid-year amendments in the Relius provision versus the QACA notice requirements in the Ascensus provision. The Relius provision allows for retroactive amendments and specifies a 30-day election period, while the Ascensus provision emphasizes a notice period of 30 to 90 days before the Plan Year and immediate eligibility notice.

relius_provision_text: Benefits may not be paid without the Participant's and the Participant's Spouse's consent if the present value of the Participant's Joint and Survivor Annuity derived from Employer and Employee contributions exceeds $5,000 and the benefit is 'immediately distributable.' However, spousal consent is not required if the distribution will be made in the form of a qualified Joint and Survivor Annuity and the benefit is 'immediately distributable.' A benefit is not distributable if any part of the benefit could be distributed to the Participant (or surviving Spouse) before the Participant attains (or would have attained if not deceased) the later of the Participant's Normal Retirement Age or age 62. Notwithstanding the foregoing, if the value of the Participant's benefit derived from Employer and Employee contributions does not exceed $5,000, then the Administrator may only distribute such benefit in a lump-sum. No distribution may be made under the preceding sentence after the Annuity Starting Date unless the Participant and the Participant's Spouse consent in writing (or in such other form as permitted by the IRS) to such distribution. Any consent required under this paragraph must be obtained not more than one-hundred eighty (180) days before commencement of the distribution and shall be made in a manner consistent with Section 6.5(a)(2). [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]

variance_notes: The Relius provision emphasizes a waiting period after providing information about annuities, whereas the Ascensus provision discusses the forms of distribution and compliance with tax regulations. The related adoption agreement elections also differ in focus, with Relius addressing partial withdrawals and hardship distributions, while Ascensus focuses on rollover contributions.

relius_provision_text: For purposes of paragraph (iii) above, an Employee who for an entire Plan Year did not have contributions made pursuant to a default election under the EACA will be treated as having not had such contributions for any prior Plan Year as well. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: The provision in (1) above will not apply to any Participant who was not employed by the Employer on the last day of the Plan Year. In addition, the provision in (1) above will not apply to any Employee included in a unit of Employees covered by an agreement which the Secretary of Labor finds to be a collective bargaining agreement between the Employer and Employee representatives if there is evidence that retirement benefits were the subject of good faith bargaining between such Employee representatives and the Employer. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions relate to contributions and employee eligibility, but they address different aspects of employee participation and eligibility criteria. The Relius provision focuses on rehired employees and their contribution history, while the Ascensus provision addresses employment status at the end of the Plan Year and collective bargaining agreements.
variance_notes: The Relius provision specifically mentions rehired employees and their contribution history, while the Ascensus provision excludes participants not employed on the last day of the Plan Year and those covered by collective bargaining agreements. This indicates a difference in focus and application.

relius_provision_text: Unless otherwise elected as provided below, a Participant who is married on the Annuity Starting Date and who does not die before the Annuity Starting Date shall receive the value of all Plan benefits in the form of a Joint and Survivor Annuity. The Joint and Survivor Annuity is an annuity that commences immediately and shall be equal in value to a single life annuity. Such joint and survivor benefits following the Participant's death shall continue to the Spouse during the Spouse's lifetime at a rate equal to either fifty percent (50%), seventy-five percent (75%) (or, sixty-six and two-thirds percent (66 2/3%) if the Insurer used to provide the annuity does not offer a joint and seventy-five percent (75%) survivor annuity), or one hundred percent (100%) of the rate at which such benefits were payable to the Participant. Unless otherwise elected in the Adoption Agreement, a joint and fifty percent (50%) survivor annuity shall be considered the designated qualified Joint and Survivor Annuity and the normal form of payment for the purposes of this Plan. However, the Participant may, without spousal consent, elect an alternative Joint and Survivor Annuity, which alternative shall be equal in value to the designated qualified Joint and Survivor Annuity. An unmarried Participant shall receive the value of such Participant's benefit in the form of a life annuity. Such unmarried Participant, however, may elect to waive the life annuity. The election must comply with the provisions of this Section as if it were an election to waive the Joint and Survivor Annuity by a married Participant, but without fulfilling the spousal consent requirement. The Participant may elect to have any annuity provided for in this Section distributed upon the attainment of the "earliest retirement age" under the Plan. The "earliest retirement age" is the earliest date on which, under the Plan, the Participant could elect to receive retirement benefits.
ascensus_provision_text: Qualified Joint and Survivor Annuity — Unless an optional form of benefit is selected pursuant to a Qualified Election within the 180-day period ending on the Annuity Starting Date, a married Participant's Vested Account Balance will be paid in the form of a Qualified Joint and Survivor Annuity and an unmarried Participant's Vested Account Balance will be paid in the form of a life annuity. The Participant may elect to have such annuity distributed upon attainment of the Earliest Retirement Age under the Plan. In the case of a married Participant, the Qualified Joint and Survivor Annuity must be at least as valuable as any other optional form of benefit payable under the Plan at the same time. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions address the Qualified Joint and Survivor Annuity for married and unmarried participants, but they differ in the specifics of elections and the context of vesting. The Relius provision includes details about spousal consent and alternative elections without spousal consent, while the Ascensus provision emphasizes the requirement for the annuity to be at least as valuable as other optional forms of benefit.
variance_notes: The Relius provision allows for alternative Joint and Survivor Annuities without spousal consent, while the Ascensus provision requires a Qualified Election within a specific timeframe. Additionally, the Ascensus provision explicitly states that the annuity must be at least as valuable as other options, which is not mentioned in the Relius provision.

relius_provision_text:
Beneficiary designation. Unless otherwise elected in the manner prescribed in Section 6.6, the Beneficiary of the Pre-Retirement Survivor Annuity shall be the Participant's surviving Spouse. Except, however, the Participant may designate a Beneficiary other than the Spouse for the Pre-Retirement Survivor Annuity if:

(1) the Participant and the Participant's Spouse have validly waived the Pre-Retirement Survivor Annuity in the manner prescribed in Section 6.6, and the Spouse has waived the right to be the Participant's Beneficiary,

(2) The Participant is legally separated or has been abandoned (within the meaning of local law) and the Participant has a court order to such effect (and there is no "qualified domestic relations order" as defined in Code §414(p) which provides otherwise),

(3) the Participant has no Spouse, or

(4) The Spouse cannot be located.

In such event, the designation of a Beneficiary shall be made on a form satisfactory to the Administrator. A Participant may at any time revoke a designation of a Beneficiary or change a Beneficiary by filing written (or in such other form as permitted by the IRS) notice of such revocation or change with the Administrator. However, the Participant's Spouse must again consent in writing (or in such other form as permitted by the IRS) to any change in Beneficiary unless the original consent acknowledged that the Spouse had the right to limit consent only to a specific Beneficiary and that the Spouse would voluntarily elected to relinquish such right.
ascensus_provision_text: Means the individual who is designated by the Participant (or the Participant's surviving Spouse) as the Beneficiary of the Participant's interest under the Plan and who is the designated beneficiary under Code section 401(a)(9) and Treasury Regulation section 1.401(a)(9)-4.
reasoning: Both provisions address beneficiary designation, but the Relius provision provides specific conditions under which a participant can designate a beneficiary other than their spouse, while the Ascensus provision is more general and does not specify conditions for designation. The Relius provision includes detailed requirements for spousal consent and circumstances under which a spouse may not be the beneficiary, which are not addressed in the Ascensus provision.
variance_notes: Relius outlines specific scenarios for beneficiary designation and spousal consent, while Ascensus defines a designated beneficiary without detailing the conditions or requirements for designation.

relius_title: SPECIAL RULE FOR CERTAIN PROFIT SHARING PLANS
relius_provision_text: If a distribution is one to which Code §§401(a)(11) and 417 do not apply, such distribution may commence less than thirty (30) days after the notice required under Regulation §1.411(a)-11(c) is given, provided that: (1) the Administrator clearly informs the Participant that the Participant has a right to a period of at least thirty (30) days after the notice to consider the decision of whether or not to elect a distribution (and, if applicable, a particular distribution option), and (2) the Participant, after receiving the notice, affirmatively elects a distribution. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text:
Unless otherwise elected in the Adoption Agreement, the safe harbor provisions of this Plan Section 5.10(E) will apply to a Participant in a profit sharing plan and will always apply to any distribution made on or after the first day of the first Plan Year beginning after December 31, 1988, from or under a separate account attributable solely to accumulated deductible employee contributions, as defined in Code section 72(o)(5)(B), and maintained on behalf of a Participant in a money purchase pension plan, if the following conditions are satisfied:

a. the Participant does not or cannot elect payments in the form of a life annuity; and

b. on the death of a Participant, the Participant's Vested Account Balance will be paid to the Participant's surviving Spouse, but if there is no surviving Spouse, or if the surviving Spouse has consented in a manner conforming to a Qualified Election, then to the Participant's Designated Beneficiary. The surviving Spouse may elect to have distribution of the Vested Account Balance commence within the 180-day period following the date of the Participant's death. The Vested Account Balance will be adjusted for gains or losses occurring after the Participant's death in accordance with the provisions of the Plan governing the adjustment of account balances for other types of distributions. This Plan Section 5.10(E) will not apply to a Participant in a profit sharing plan if the plan is a direct or indirect transferee of a defined benefit plan, money purchase pension plan, a target benefit pension plan, stock bonus, or profit sharing plan that is subject to the survivor annuity requirements of Code sections 401(a)(11) and 417. If this Plan Section 5.10(E) applies, then no other provisions of this Plan Section 5.10 will apply as provided in Treasury Regulations. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions relate to distributions from profit sharing plans, but they govern different aspects and conditions of those distributions. The Relius provision focuses on the timing of distributions and participant rights, while the Ascensus provision outlines safe harbor conditions and beneficiary distribution rules.
variance_notes: Key differences include the Relius provision's emphasis on participant notice and election rights, whereas the Ascensus provision specifies conditions for beneficiary distributions and does not address participant election timing.

relius_title: SPECIAL RULE FOR CERTAIN PROFIT SHARING PLANS
relius_provision_text: If an election is made to offer life annuities as a form of distribution but not as the normal form of distribution, then the Joint and Survivor Annuity provisions of Section 6.5 shall not apply if a Participant does not elect an annuity form of distribution. Furthermore, Subsection (e) shall not apply if a Participant elects an annuity form of distribution. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text:
Unless otherwise elected in the Adoption Agreement, the safe harbor provisions of this Plan Section 5.10(E) will apply to a Participant in a profit sharing plan and will always apply to any distribution made on or after the first day of the first Plan Year beginning after December 31, 1988, from or under a separate account attributable solely to accumulated deductible employee contributions, as defined in Code section 72(o)(5)(B), and maintained on behalf of a Participant in a money purchase pension plan, if the following conditions are satisfied:

a. the Participant does not or cannot elect payments in the form of a life annuity; and

b. on the death of a Participant, the Participant's Vested Account Balance will be paid to the Participant's surviving Spouse, but if there is no surviving Spouse, or if the surviving Spouse has consented in a manner conforming to a Qualified Election, then to the Participant's Designated Beneficiary. The surviving Spouse may elect to have distribution of the Vested Account Balance commence within the 180-day period following the date of the Participant's death. The Vested Account Balance will be adjusted for gains or losses occurring after the Participant's death in accordance with the provisions of the Plan governing the adjustment of account balances for other types of distributions. This Plan Section 5.10(E) will not apply to a Participant in a profit sharing plan if the plan is a direct or indirect transferee of a defined benefit plan, money purchase pension plan, a target benefit pension plan, stock bonus, or profit sharing plan that is subject to the survivor annuity requirements of Code sections 401(a)(11) and 417. If this Plan Section 5.10(E) applies, then no other provisions of this Plan Section 5.10 will apply as provided in Treasury Regulations. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions address distribution options related to profit sharing plans, but they focus on different aspects. The Relius provision specifically discusses the applicability of Joint and Survivor Annuity provisions based on the election of annuity forms, while the Ascensus provision outlines safe harbor provisions for distributions and conditions related to surviving spouses. The focus on life annuities and the conditions for distributions differ significantly.
variance_notes: Relius emphasizes the election of annuity forms and the implications for Joint and Survivor Annuity provisions, whereas Ascensus details safe harbor provisions and survivor benefits, indicating a different approach to participant distributions.

relius_title: SPECIAL RULE FOR CERTAIN PROFIT SHARING PLANS
relius_provision_text: If an election is made to not offer life annuities as a form of distribution, then a Participant shall be prohibited from electing benefits in the form of a life annuity and the Joint and Survivor Annuity provisions of Section 6.5 shall not apply. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text:
Unless otherwise elected in the Adoption Agreement, the safe harbor provisions of this Plan Section 5.10(E) will apply to a Participant in a profit sharing plan and will always apply to any distribution made on or after the first day of the first Plan Year beginning after December 31, 1988, from or under a separate account attributable solely to accumulated deductible employee contributions, as defined in Code section 72(o)(5)(B), and maintained on behalf of a Participant in a money purchase pension plan, if the following conditions are satisfied:

a. the Participant does not or cannot elect payments in the form of a life annuity; and

b. on the death of a Participant, the Participant's Vested Account Balance will be paid to the Participant's surviving Spouse, but if there is no surviving Spouse, or if the surviving Spouse has consented in a manner conforming to a Qualified Election, then to the Participant's Designated Beneficiary. The surviving Spouse may elect to have distribution of the Vested Account Balance commence within the 180-day period following the date of the Participant's death. The Vested Account Balance will be adjusted for gains or losses occurring after the Participant's death in accordance with the provisions of the Plan governing the adjustment of account balances for other types of distributions. This Plan Section 5.10(E) will not apply to a Participant in a profit sharing plan if the plan is a direct or indirect transferee of a defined benefit plan, money purchase pension plan, a target benefit pension plan, stock bonus, or profit sharing plan that is subject to the survivor annuity requirements of Code sections 401(a)(11) and 417. If this Plan Section 5.10(E) applies, then no other provisions of this Plan Section 5.10 will apply as provided in Treasury Regulations. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions address the treatment of life annuities in the context of profit sharing plans, but they do so in different contexts and with different implications. The Relius provision explicitly prohibits the election of life annuities if not offered, while the Ascensus provision discusses conditions under which safe harbor provisions apply, including the treatment of a surviving spouse's benefits. The focus and implications of the provisions differ significantly.
variance_notes: Relius focuses on the prohibition of life annuities and the applicability of Joint and Survivor Annuity provisions, while Ascensus outlines safe harbor provisions and the treatment of vested account balances upon a participant's death, indicating a broader scope of beneficiary treatment.

relius_provision_text: Notwithstanding Subsection (e)(1) above, if elected in the Adoption Agreement, a Participant who performs service in the uniformed services (as defined in Code §414(u)(12)(B)) on active duty for a period of more than thirty (30) days, the Participant will be deemed to have a severance from employment solely for purposes of eligibility for distributions under this Plan. The Plan will not distribute such deemed severance unless the Participant specifically elects to receive a benefit distribution hereunder. If a Participant elects to receive a distribution of such deemed severance, and a distribution on account of another Plan provision (such as a "qualified reservist distribution" defined in Subsection (b)) then the other Plan provision will control and the six (6) month suspension will not apply. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions address distributions related to deemed severance from employment due to service in the uniformed services, but they govern different aspects of eligibility and distribution rules. The Relius provision focuses on eligibility for distributions, while the Ascensus provision outlines specific distribution events and conditions.
variance_notes: The Relius provision allows for deemed severance solely for eligibility for distributions, while the Ascensus provision includes deemed severance as one of several distribution events and imposes a six-month suspension on contributions following such a distribution. Additionally, the Ascensus provision includes more detailed options for distributions that are not present in the Relius provision.

relius_provision_text: Frozen Plans. The Employer may designate that the Plan is a frozen Plan at the Contribution Types Section of the Adoption Agreement. As a frozen Plan, the Employer will not make any Employer contributions with respect to Compensation earned after the date the Plan is frozen, and if the Plan is a 401(k) Plan, no Participant will be permitted to make Elective Deferrals to the Plan for any period following such date. In addition, once a Plan is frozen, no additional Employees shall become Participants. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: Unless otherwise elected in the Adoption Agreement, if the Plan is a 401(k) profit sharing plan, Employer Prevailing Wage Contributions will be designated as Qualified Nonelective Contributions. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions relate to employer contributions in a retirement plan context, but they govern different aspects. The Relius provision specifically addresses frozen plans and the cessation of contributions and participant eligibility, while the Ascensus provision discusses employer contributions in the context of a 401(k) profit sharing plan without mentioning freezing or cessation of contributions. The options available in each are not directly comparable as they pertain to different scenarios.
variance_notes: Relius focuses on frozen plans and the implications of freezing on contributions and participation, whereas Ascensus discusses contributions without addressing freezing or cessation of contributions. Additionally, the specific elections available differ significantly.

ascensus_provision_text: Loans must be adequately secured and bear a reasonable interest rate. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions address loans to participants, but the Relius provision is more detailed, outlining specific requirements for the loan program, including administration, application procedures, approval criteria, limitations, interest rates, collateral, and default events. The Ascensus provision is more general and lacks the detailed structure found in the Relius provision.
variance_notes: The Relius provision includes specific requirements for the loan program's administration and procedures, while the Ascensus provision only states that loans must be adequately secured and bear a reasonable interest rate without further elaboration on the loan program's structure or administration.

relius_provision_text: Subject to the consent of the Trustee (or Insurer), the Employer may make its contribution to the Plan in the form of unencumbered property instead of cash, provided the contribution of property is not a prohibited transaction. The decision to make a contribution of property is subject to the general fiduciary rules under the Act. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: If the Plan is a profit sharing plan, Employer Prevailing Wage Contributions will be designated as Employer Profit Sharing Contributions. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions relate to employer contributions in a profit sharing plan, but they address different aspects. The Relius provision allows for contributions in the form of unencumbered property, while the Ascensus provision focuses on designating Prevailing Wage Contributions as Profit Sharing Contributions. The Relius provision includes fiduciary considerations and prohibits transactions, which are not mentioned in the Ascensus provision.
variance_notes: Key differences include the Relius provision's allowance for property contributions and fiduciary rules, which are absent in the Ascensus provision. Additionally, the Ascensus provision does not provide details on the nature of contributions beyond designation.

relius_provision_text: Recognition of predecessor service. Unless specifically provided otherwise in the Adoption Agreement, an Eligible Employee who satisfies the Plan's eligibility requirements by reason of recognition of service with a predecessor employer will become a Participant as of the day the Plan credits service with the predecessor employer or, if later, the date the Employee would have otherwise entered the Plan had the Employee been continuously employed by the Employer. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: Means the aggregate of all time periods beginning on the Employee’s date of hire or rehire and ending on the date a Break in Service begins. The first day of employment or reemployment is the first day the Employee performs an Hour of Service. If the Plan is using the Elapsed Time method of determining eligibility service, this definition of Period of Service will replace the definitions of Year of Eligibility Service found in this Definitions section of the Plan. If the Plan is using the Elapsed Time method of determining vesting service, this definition of Period of Service will replace the definition of Year of Vesting Service found in this Definitions section of the Plan. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions relate to eligibility for participation in a retirement plan, but they focus on different aspects. The Relius provision specifically addresses recognition of predecessor service, while the Ascensus provision defines 'Period of Service' in terms of time periods related to employment. They do not govern the same feature directly.
variance_notes: The Relius provision emphasizes predecessor service recognition and its impact on eligibility, whereas the Ascensus provision focuses on the calculation of service time for eligibility. The options available in the adoption agreements also differ in their focus and implications for eligibility.

relius_provision_text: General rule. An Eligible Employee who has satisfied the conditions of eligibility pursuant to Section 3.1 shall become a Participant effective as of the date elected in the Adoption Agreement. Regardless of any election in the Adoption Agreement to the contrary, an Eligible Employee who has satisfied the maximum age (21) and service requirements (one (1) Year or (Period) of Service (or, with respect to contributions other than Employee Deferrals, more than one (1) year if full administrative vesting) and who is otherwise entitled to participate, will become a Participant no later than the earlier of (1) six (6) months after such requirements are satisfied, or (2) the first day of the first Plan Year after such requirements are satisfied, unless the Employee separates from service before such participation date. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: The Plan Administrator will determine the eligibility of each Employee to be a Participant. This determination will be conclusive and binding upon all persons except as otherwise provided herein or by law. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions address eligibility for participation in a retirement plan, but they do so in different ways. The Relius provision specifies conditions under which an Eligible Employee becomes a Participant, including age and service requirements, while the Ascensus provision grants the Plan Administrator the authority to determine eligibility, which is more general and does not specify conditions.
variance_notes: The Relius provision includes specific eligibility criteria (age and service) and a timeline for participation, whereas the Ascensus provision focuses on the Plan Administrator's discretion without detailing specific eligibility conditions.

relius_provision_text: Claims for benefits under the Plan may be filed in writing with the Administrator. Written or electronic notice of the disposition of a claim shall be furnished to the claimant within ninety (90) days (45 days if the claim involves disability benefits and disability is not based on the Social Security Acts) after the application is filed, or such period as required by applicable law or Department of Labor regulation. Any electronic notification shall comply with the standards imposed by Department of Labor Regulation §2520.104b-1(c)(1)(i), (iii) and (iv) or any subsequent guidance. In the event the claim is denied, the reasons for the denial shall be specifically set forth in the notice in language calculated to be understood by the claimant, pertinent provisions of the Plan shall be cited, and, where appropriate, an explanation as to how the claimant can perfect the claim will be provided. In addition, the claimant shall be furnished with an explanation of the Plan's claims review procedure.
ascensus_provision_text: Remedies Available — The Participant or Beneficiary will have 60 days from receipt of the denial notice in which to make written application for review by the Plan Administrator. The Participant or Beneficiary may request that the review be in the nature of a hearing. The Participant or Beneficiary will have the right to representation, to review pertinent documents, and to submit comments in writing (or in any other form permitted by the IRS or DOL). The Plan Administrator shall issue a decision on such review within 60 days after receipt of an application for review as provided for in this Plan Section 5.09 and pursuant to Department of Labor regulation Section 2560.503-1. If the claim involves disability benefits under the Plan, the Participant or Beneficiary will have 180 days from receipt of the denial notice in which to make written application for review by the Plan Administrator. The Plan Administrator shall issue a decision on such review within 45 days after receipt of an application for review as provided for in this Plan Section 5.09 and pursuant to Labor regulation Section 2560.503-1. Upon a decision unfavorable to the Participant or Beneficiary, such Participant or Beneficiary will be entitled to bring such actions in law or equity as may be necessary or appropriate to protect or clarify their right to benefits under this Plan. The Participant or Beneficiary will have one year from receipt of the denial notice to bring such action.
reasoning: Both provisions relate to claims for benefits under a retirement plan, but they govern different aspects of the claims process. The Relius provision focuses on the initial claim filing and the timeline for notification of the claim's disposition, while the Ascensus provision addresses the review process after a claim has been denied, including timelines for appeals and rights of the participant or beneficiary.
variance_notes: Key differences include the focus on initial claims in the Relius provision versus the appeals process in the Ascensus provision. Additionally, the timelines for responses differ, with Relius specifying 90 days (or 45 for disability) for initial claims, while Ascensus specifies 60 days for standard appeals and 45 days for disability appeals.

relius_provision_text: If elected in the Adoption Agreement, this Plan is intended to be a SIMPLE 401(k) plan which satisfies the requirements of Code §§401(k)(1) and 401(m)(10).
ascensus_provision_text: Notwithstanding the preceding, the ADP test described above is treated as satisfied for any SIMPLE 401(k) Year in which an Eligible Employer maintains this Plan as a SIMPLE 401(k) Plan. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions relate to SIMPLE 401(k) plans, but they address different aspects. The Relius provision states the plan's intention to be a SIMPLE 401(k) plan, while the Ascensus provision discusses the ADP test satisfaction for SIMPLE 401(k) years. They do not govern the same feature directly.
variance_notes: The Relius provision focuses on the plan's classification, whereas the Ascensus provision deals with compliance testing related to contributions. The options/elections in the Ascensus provision pertain to participant eligibility and allocation, which are not addressed in the Relius provision.

relius_provision_text: (b) Administrator's discretion/nondiscriminatory administration. The Administrator has total and complete discretion to interpret and construe the Plan and to determine all questions arising in the administration, interpretation and application of the Plan. Any determination the Administrator makes under the Plan is final and binding upon any affected person. The Administrator must exercise all of its Plan powers and discretion, and perform all of its duties, in a uniform and nondiscriminatory manner.
ascensus_title: POWERS AND DUTIES OF THE PLAN ADMINISTRATOR
ascensus_provision_text: The Plan Administrator will have the authority to control and manage the operation and administration of the Plan. The Plan Administrator shall administer the Plan for the exclusive benefit of the Participants and their Beneficiaries in accordance with the specific terms of the Plan.
reasoning: Both provisions address the authority and discretion of the Plan Administrator, but they emphasize different aspects. The Relius provision highlights the Administrator's discretion and the requirement for nondiscriminatory administration, while the Ascensus provision focuses on the exclusive benefit of Participants and their Beneficiaries. This indicates a difference in emphasis and scope.
variance_notes: The Relius provision includes a specific requirement for nondiscriminatory administration and finality of decisions, which is not explicitly mentioned in the Ascensus provision. The Ascensus provision emphasizes the exclusive benefit of Participants, which is not a focus in the Relius text.

relius_title: Exception for certain debts to Plan.
relius_provision_text: (d) Exception for certain debts to Plan. Notwithstanding any provision of this Section to the contrary, an offset to a Participant's accrued benefit against an amount that the Participant is ordered or required to pay the Plan with respect to a judgment, order, or decree issued, or a settlement entered into, on or after August 5, 1997, shall be permitted in accordance with Code §§401(a)(13)(C) and (D). [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text:
A Domestic Relations Order will not be treated as failing to meet the requirements above solely because such order requires that payment of benefits be made to an Alternate Payee

1. on or after the date on which the Participant attains (or would have attained) the earliest retirement age as defined in Code section 414(p)(4)(B),

2. as if the Participant had retired on the date on which such payment is to begin under such order, and

3. in any form in which such benefits may be paid under the Plan to the Participant (other than in a Qualified Joint and Survivor Annuity) with respect to the Alternate Payee and their subsequent spouse. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions address exceptions related to payments from a retirement plan, but they focus on different aspects. The Relius provision specifically discusses offsets for debts to the Plan due to legal orders, while the Ascensus provision pertains to the treatment of Domestic Relations Orders and payments to Alternate Payees. They do not govern the same plan feature/requirement.
variance_notes: The Relius provision is about offsets for participant debts, while the Ascensus provision is about payments to Alternate Payees under Domestic Relations Orders. The options/elections available in the adoption agreements also differ significantly in focus.

relius_provision_text: Delay in processing transactions. Notwithstanding anything in this Section to the contrary, all information necessary to properly reflect a given transaction may not be available until after the date specified herein for processing such transaction, in which case the transaction will be reflected when such information is received and processed. Subject to express limits that may be imposed under the Code, the processing of any contribution, distribution or other transaction may be delayed for any legitimate business reason (including, but not limited to, failure of systems or computer programs, failure of the means of the transmission of data, force majeure, the failure of a service provider to timely receive values or prices, and correction for errors or omissions or the errors or omissions of any service provider). The processing date of a transaction will be binding for all purposes of the Plan. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: For all purposes under the Plan other than eligibility, contribution, allocation, and vesting determinations (e.g., testing and distribution eligibility), Employer Prevailing Wage Contributions will be designated as follows. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions relate to employer contributions, but they focus on different aspects. The Relius provision discusses delays in processing transactions related to contributions, while the Ascensus provision specifies designations for Employer Prevailing Wage Contributions without addressing processing delays or transaction handling.
variance_notes: The Relius provision includes details about transaction processing delays and legitimate business reasons for such delays, which are not present in the Ascensus provision. Additionally, the Ascensus provision focuses on designating contributions for specific purposes, which is not covered in the Relius text.

relius_provision_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, with respect to an Employer which is not a tax-exempt entity, the Employer's contribution for any Fiscal Year shall not exceed the maximum amount allowable as a deduction to the Employer under Code §404. However, to the extent necessary to provide the top-heavy minimum allocations, the Employer shall make a contribution even if it exceeds the amount that is deductible under Code §404. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: If the Plan is a money purchase pension plan, Employer Prevailing Wage Contributions will be designated as Employer Money Purchase Pension Plan Contributions. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions relate to employer contributions in a Money Purchase Plan context, but they address different aspects. The Relius provision specifies limits on contributions based on tax deductibility and top-heavy minimums, while the Ascensus provision focuses on designating contributions as Money Purchase Pension Plan Contributions without addressing deductibility or top-heavy requirements.
variance_notes: Key differences include Relius's emphasis on tax deductibility limits and top-heavy minimums, which are not mentioned in the Ascensus provision. Additionally, the Ascensus provision does not provide details on the contribution limits or requirements for employer contributions.

relius_provision_text: Contract conversion at retirement. Subject to the survivor annuity requirements of Sections 6.5 and 6.6 (if applicable), the Administrator must direct the Trustee (or Insurer) to distribute the Contracts to the Participant or convert the entire value of the Contracts at or before retirement into cash or provide for a periodic income so that no portion of such value may be used to continue life insurance protection beyond the Participant's actual retirement date.
ascensus_title: Conversion of Life Insurance Contracts Upon Separation
ascensus_provision_text: Subject to Plan Section 5.10, the contracts on a Participant's life will be converted to cash or an annuity distributed to the Participant upon separation from service with the Employer. In addition, contracts on the joint lives of a Participant and another person may not be maintained under this Plan if such Participant ceases to have an insurable interest in such other person. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions address the conversion of life insurance contracts, but they apply to different events (retirement vs. separation from service). The Relius provision specifies requirements related to retirement and survivor annuity, while the Ascensus provision focuses on separation from service and insurable interest.
variance_notes: Key differences include the triggering events for conversion (retirement vs. separation) and the additional stipulation in the Ascensus provision regarding insurable interest, which is not mentioned in the Relius provision.

relius_provision_text: Notwithstanding the preceding, the limitations imposed herein with respect to the purchase of life insurance shall not apply, in the case of a Profit Sharing Plan (including a 401(k) Plan), to the portion of the Participant's Account, other than the Participant's Elective Deferral Account, Qualified Matching Account and Qualified Nonelective Contribution Account, that has accumulated for at least two (2) Plan Years or to the entire Participant's Account if the Participant has been a Participant in the Plan for at least five (5) years. In addition, amounts transferred to this Plan in accordance with Section 4.6(f)(1)(ii) or iii) (and a Participant's Voluntary Contribution Account) may be used to purchase Contracts without limitation. Thus, amounts that are not subject to the limitations contained herein may be used to purchase life insurance on any person in whom a Participant has an insurable interest or on the joint lives of a Participant and any person in whom the Participant has an insurable interest, and without regard to the amount of premiums paid to purchase any life insurance hereunder. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: If the Employer elects to take Qualified Matching Contributions into account as Elective Deferrals for purposes of the ADP test, then (subject to such other requirements as may be prescribed by the Secretary of the Treasury) the Employer may elect, in a uniform and nondiscriminatory manner, to either include all Qualified Matching Contributions that are needed to meet the ADP test or to include only the amount of such Qualified Matching Contributions that are needed to meet the ADP test. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions relate to matching contributions in retirement plans, but they address different aspects. The Relius provision focuses on the limitations regarding the purchase of life insurance with certain account balances, while the Ascensus provision discusses the treatment of Qualified Matching Contributions for ADP testing purposes. They do not govern the same plan feature directly.
variance_notes: The Relius provision includes specific conditions for life insurance purchases based on account duration and participant tenure, which is not addressed in the Ascensus provision. The Ascensus provision is more focused on compliance with ADP testing and does not mention life insurance.

relius_provision_text: A non-Spouse Beneficiary who is a "designated Beneficiary" under Code § 401(a)(9)(E) and the Regulations thereunder, by a direct trustee-to-trustee transfer ("direct rollover"), may roll over all or any portion an "eligible rollover distribution" to an IRA the Beneficiary establishes for purposes of receiving the distribution. If the Participant's named Beneficiary is a trust, the Plan may make a direct rollover to an IRA on behalf of the trust, provided the trust satisfies the requirements to be a "designated Beneficiary." [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: Means, for purposes of the Direct Rollover provisions of the Plan, an individual retirement account described in Code sections 408(a) or 408A, an individual retirement annuity described in Code section 408(b), a SIMPLE IRA described in Code section 408(p), an annuity plan described in Code section 403(a), an annuity contract described in Code section 403(b), an eligible plan under Code section 457(b) that is maintained by a state, political subdivision of a state, or an agency or instrumentality of a state or political subdivision of a state (and that agrees to separately account for amounts transferred into such plan from this Plan), or a qualified plan described in Code section 401(a) that accepts the Recipient’s Eligible Rollover Distribution. The definition of Eligible Retirement Plan will also apply to the case of a distribution to a surviving Spouse, or to a Spouse or former Spouse who is the Alternate Payee under a Qualified Domestic Relations Order, as defined in Code section 414(p). If any portion of an Eligible Rollover Distribution is attributable to payments or distributions from a designated Roth account, an Eligible Rollover Distribution will include only another designated Roth account of the individual from whose account the payments or distributions were made, or a Roth IRA of such individual. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions relate to eligibility for rollovers, but they focus on different aspects. The Relius provision specifically addresses non-spouse beneficiary rollover rights, while the Ascensus provision defines what constitutes an eligible retirement plan for direct rollovers. They are related but not semantically equivalent.
variance_notes: The Relius provision is specific to non-spouse beneficiaries and their rollover rights, while the Ascensus provision provides a broader definition of eligible retirement plans for direct rollovers, including provisions for surviving spouses and alternate payees.

relius_provision_text: "Beneficiary" means the person (or entity) to whom all or a portion of a deceased Participant's interest in the Plan is, or may become, payable upon the Participant's death as identified in records maintained by the Plan, subject to the restrictions of Sections 6.2 and 6.6. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: Means an Employee or former Employee. In addition, the Employee's or former Employee's surviving Spouse and the Employee's or former Employee's Spouse or former Spouse who is the Alternate Payer under a Qualified Domestic Relations Order, as defined in Code section 414(p), are Recipients with regard to the interest of the Spouse or former Spouse. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: While both provisions relate to compensation and beneficiaries, they govern different aspects. The Relius provision focuses on the definition of a 'Beneficiary' in the context of a deceased Participant's interest, while the Ascensus provision defines 'Recipient' in relation to Employees and their Spouses or former Spouses under specific circumstances. The options available in the adoption agreements also pertain to different features of the plans.
variance_notes: Key differences include the focus on death benefits in the Relius provision versus the broader definition of Recipients in the Ascensus provision, which includes surviving Spouses and former Spouses under QDROs. The options in the adoption agreements are not directly comparable as they pertain to different plan features.

relius_title: ELECTION TO BECOME A PARTICIPATING EMPLOYER
relius_provision_text: Notwithstanding anything herein to the contrary, with the consent of the Employer, any Employer may adopt the Employer's Plan and all of the provisions herein, and participate herein and be known as a Participating Employer, by a properly executed document evidencing said intent and will of such Participating Employer (a participation agreement). In the event a Participating Employer is not an Affiliated Employer, then the provisions of Article XIV shall apply rather than the provision of this Article XI. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: The Plan Administrator will determine the eligibility of each Employee to be a Participant. This determination will be conclusive and binding upon all persons except as otherwise provided herein or by law. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions address eligibility for participation in a retirement plan, but they do so in different ways. The Relius provision focuses on the process for an employer to become a Participating Employer and the conditions under which eligibility is determined, while the Ascensus provision emphasizes the authority of the Plan Administrator to determine eligibility, which is binding. The options available in the adoption agreements also differ in focus, with Relius addressing modifications to eligibility conditions and Ascensus discussing specific employee classifications and contributions.
variance_notes: Key differences include the Relius provision's emphasis on the participation agreement and conditions for non-affiliated employers, while the Ascensus provision centers on the Plan Administrator's determinations and specific employee classifications.

relius_provision_text: (b) Permissible amendments. The Employer may amend the Plan to accomplish any of the following items without affecting reliance on the opinion letter: (1) change the choice of options in the Adoption Agreement or Appendix A to the Adoption Agreement (Special Effective Dates and Other Permitted Elections) , (2) add certain sample or model amendments published by the Internal Revenue Service or other required good-faith amendments where the IRS has provided that their adoption will not cause the Plan to be treated as an individually designed plan, (3) add a list of any "Section 411(d)(6) protected benefits" which must be preserved, (4) adjust the limitations under Code §§415, 402(g), 401(a)(3) and 414(p) to reflect annual cost-of-living increases, and (5) change the pre-approved plan Provider's name. An Employer that amends the Plan for any other reason, including a waiver of the minimum funding requirement under Code §412(c), will no longer participate in the pre-approved Plan program for purposes of reliance on an Opinion Letter, the Provider will no longer have the authority to amend the Plan on behalf of the Employer as of the date (1) the Employer amends the Plan to incorporate a type of Plan described in section 6.03 of Rev. Proc. 2017-41 that is not permitted under the Pre-approved Plan program, or (2) the Internal Revenue Service notifies the Employer in accordance with section 8.06(3) of Rev. Proc. 2017-41, that the Plan is an individually designed plan due to the nature and extent of Employer amendments to the Plan. For purposes of this Subsection, the mass submitter shall be recognized as the agent of the Provider. If the Provider does not adopt the amendments made by the mass submitter, it will no longer be identical to or a minor modifier of the mass submitter plan. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: A. Right of Pre-approved Document Provider to Amend the Plan or Terminate Sponsorship 2. An amendment by the Pre-approved Document Provider will be accomplished by giving notice (either in writing or in any other form permitted under rules promulgated by the IRS and DOL) to the Adopting Employer of the amendment to be made. The notice will set forth the text of such amendment and the date such amendment is to be effective. Such amendment will take effect unless within the 30-day period after such notice is provided, or within such other period as the notice may specify, adopting Employer gives the Pre-approved Document Provider written notice of refusal to consent to the amendment. Such written notice of refusal will have the effect of withdrawing the Plan as a pre-approved plan and will cause the Plan to be considered an individually designed plan. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions address amendments to the retirement plan, but they differ significantly in their approach and implications. The Relius provision outlines permissible amendments that do not affect reliance on the opinion letter, while the Ascensus provision describes the process for amendments initiated by the provider and the consequences of refusal by the employer. The options available in the related adoption agreements also differ in focus, with Relius emphasizing employer nonelective contributions and Ascensus focusing on eligibility and allocation formulas.
variance_notes: Key differences include the Relius provision's focus on permissible amendments without affecting the opinion letter and the Ascensus provision's emphasis on the provider's right to amend and the employer's ability to refuse. The related adoption agreement elections also differ in content and focus.

relius_provision_text:
(a) General rules

(1) Effective Date. Subject to the Joint and Survivor Annuity requirements set forth in Plan Section 6.5, the requirements of this Section shall apply to any distribution of a Participant's interest in the Plan and will take precedence over any inconsistent provisions of this Plan.

(2) Requirements of Treasury Regulations incorporated. All distributions required under this Section will be determined and made in accordance with the Regulations under Code §401(a)(9) and the minimum distribution incidental benefit requirement of Code §401(a)(9)(G). [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_title: Amount of Required Minimum Distribution for Each Distribution Calendar Year
ascensus_provision_text:
1. Amount of Required Minimum Distribution for Each Distribution Calendar Year – During the Participant’s lifetime, the minimum amount that will be distributed for each Distribution Calendar Year is the lesser of:
  a. the quotient obtained by dividing the Participant’s Benefit by the distribution period in the Uniform Lifetime Table set forth in Treasury Regulation section 1.401(a)(9)-9, Q&A 2, using the Participant’s age as of the Participant’s birthday in the Distribution Calendar Year; or
  b. if the Participant’s sole Designated Beneficiary for the Distribution Calendar Year is the Participant’s Spouse, the quotient obtained by dividing the Participant’s Benefit by the number in the Joint and Last Survivor Table set forth in Treasury Regulation section 1.401(a)(9)-9, Q&A 3, using the Participant’s and Spouse’s attained ages as of the Participant’s and Spouse’s birthdays in the Distribution Calendar Year.

Additionally, the minimum amount that will be distributed for each Distribution Calendar Year will also include any payments from a Qualifying Longevity Annuity Contract where payments have commenced. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions address required minimum distributions (RMDs) but differ in their specific calculations and contexts. The Relius provision outlines general rules and compliance with Code §401(a)(9), while the Ascensus provision provides detailed calculations for RMDs based on specific tables and scenarios.
variance_notes: The Relius provision is more general and focuses on compliance with regulations, whereas the Ascensus provision specifies how to calculate the RMD based on the participant's age and the type of beneficiary. Additionally, the related adoption agreement elections differ significantly in focus, with Relius addressing partial withdrawals and hardship distributions, while Ascensus focuses on rollover contributions.

relius_provision_text: Every Participant may terminate employment with the Employer and retire for purposes hereof on the Participant's Normal Retirement Date or Early Retirement Date. However, a Participant may postpone the severence of employment with the Employer to a later date, in which event the participation of such Participant in the Plan, including the right to receive allocations pursuant to Section 4.3, shall continue until such Participant's Retirement Date. Upon a Participant's Retirement Date, or if elected in the Adoption Agreement, the attainment of Normal Retirement Date without severance of employment with the Employer (subject to Sections 6.11 and 12.2(e)), or as soon thereafter as is practicable, the Administrator shall direct the distribution, at the election of the Participant (unless a distribution is mandatory under the other terms of the Plan), of the Participant's entire Vested interest in the Plan in accordance with Section 6.5. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: The Plan Administrator will determine the eligibility of each Employee to be a Participant. This determination will be conclusive and binding upon all persons except as otherwise provided herein or by law. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions address eligibility for participation in a retirement plan, but they focus on different aspects. The Relius provision specifically discusses retirement dates and the process for distribution upon retirement, while the Ascensus provision emphasizes the Plan Administrator's role in determining eligibility, which is more general and does not directly address retirement or distribution.
variance_notes: The Relius provision includes specific details about retirement dates and distributions, while the Ascensus provision is more focused on the administrative determination of eligibility without detailing retirement conditions or distribution processes.

relius_provision_text: The Administrator shall direct the Trustee (or Insurer), as of each Valuation Date, to determine the net worth of the assets comprising the Trust Fund as it exists on the Valuation Date. In determining such net worth, the Trustee (or Insurer) shall value the assets comprising the Trust Fund at their fair market value as of the Valuation Date and may deduct all expenses for which the Trustee has not yet been paid by the Employer or the Trust Fund. The Trustee (or Insurer) shall, when determining the net worth of the assets, may update the value of any shares held in a Participant Directed Account by reference to the number of shares held on behalf of the Participant, priced at the market value as of the Valuation Date. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text:
By adopting this Plan, the Employer establishes the Fund, which will consist of the assets of the Plan held by the Trustee (or Custodian, if applicable). Assets within the Fund may be pooled on behalf of all Participants, earmarked on behalf of each Participant, or be a combination of pooled and earmarked assets. To the extent that assets are earmarked for a particular Participant, they will be held in a Separate Fund for that Participant.

No part of the corpus or income of the Fund may be used for, or diverted to, purposes other than for the exclusive benefit of Participants or their Beneficiaries. The Fund will be valued each Valuation Date at fair market value. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions address the valuation of trust fund assets, but they focus on different aspects of fund establishment and maintenance. The Relius provision specifically discusses the valuation of the Trust Fund and the deduction of expenses, while the Ascensus provision emphasizes the establishment of the Fund and the exclusive benefit of Participants. They do not govern the same plan feature in a direct manner.
variance_notes: The Relius provision is more focused on the valuation process and the treatment of expenses, whereas the Ascensus provision discusses the structure of the Fund and its purpose. The related adoption agreement elections also differ in focus, with Relius addressing employer contributions and Ascensus focusing on participant eligibility and allocation formulas.

relius_provision_text: (e) Used to provide benefits. At Normal Retirement Date, or such other date when the Participant or the Participant's Beneficiary is entitled to receive benefits, the Participant's Voluntary Contribution Account shall be used to provide additional benefits to the Participant or the Participant's Beneficiary. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: Except as otherwise elected in the Adoption Agreement, the Employer may contribute an amount to be determined from year to year. Unless otherwise elected in the Adoption Agreement, if this Plan is a profit sharing plan, the Employer may, in its sole discretion, make contributions without regard to current or accumulated earnings or profits. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions relate to employer contributions, but they govern different aspects. The Relius provision specifically addresses after-tax voluntary employee contributions and their use at retirement, while the Ascensus provision discusses the employer's discretion to contribute to a profit-sharing plan without regard to earnings. The focus and implications of the contributions differ significantly.
variance_notes: Relius focuses on employee contributions and their benefits at retirement, while Ascensus emphasizes employer contributions and discretion in a profit-sharing context.

relius_provision_text: "Fiduciary" means any person who (a) exercises any discretionary authority or discretionary control respecting management of the Plan or exercises any authority or control respecting management or disposition of its assets, (b) renders investment advice for a fee or other compensation, direct or indirect, with respect to any monies or other property of the Plan or has any authority or responsibility to do so, or (c) has any discretionary authority or discretionary responsibility to do so in the administration of the Plan. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: Means a person who exercises any discretionary authority or control with respect to management of the Plan, renders investment advice as defined in ERISA section 3(21), or has any discretionary authority or responsibility regarding the administration of the Plan. The Employer and such other individuals either appointed by the Employer or deemed to be fiduciaries as a result of their actions shall serve as Fiduciaries under this Plan and fulfill the fiduciary responsibilities described in Part 4, Title I of ERISA including discharging their duties with respect to the Plan solely in the interest of the Participants and Beneficiaries and with the undivided loyalty and diligence under the circumstances prevailing at that prudent man acting in a like capacity and familiar with such matters would use in the conduct of an enterprise of a like character and with like aims. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions define 'Fiduciary' in the context of a retirement plan, but they differ in their specific language and scope. The Relius provision includes specific references to compensation types and post-severance compensation, while the Ascensus provision emphasizes fiduciary responsibilities under ERISA and does not mention compensation types. The related adoption agreement elections also differ significantly, indicating that they govern different aspects of the plan.
variance_notes: The Relius provision includes specific compensation-related options, while the Ascensus provision focuses on fiduciary duties and responsibilities without similar options. This indicates a difference in the plan features they govern.
